original_hypothesis.title: nextWithdrawal Index Corruption When releaseManagedNFT Called During Partial Withdrawal Cycle
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: releaseManagedNFT uses swap-and-pop on ManagedNFTs but does not adjust the nextWithdrawal index. If an NFT at index < nextWithdrawal is released during a partial withdrawal cycle, the element swapped from the end (which hasn't been withdrawn from yet) replaces the removed element and is skipped. Worse, if enough NFTs are released such that ManagedNFTs.length < nextWithdrawal, the completion check `nextWithdrawal == ManagedNFTs.length` never triggers (nextWithdrawal > ManagedNFTs.length), and nextWithdrawal is never reset to 0, permanently stalling the withdrawal cycle.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The interaction between ManagedNFTs array modification and the withdrawal cursor is a multi-function state flow issue. The code does not guard against ManagedNFTs modification while nextWithdrawal > 0.

proof_of_signal.pattern: releaseManagedNFT swap-and-pop does not adjust nextWithdrawal; withdrawFromManagedNFTs at lines 238-257 relies on nextWithdrawal == ManagedNFTs.length for completion

preconditions: Owner calls releaseManagedNFT for an NFT at index < nextWithdrawal
Or enough NFTs released that ManagedNFTs.length < nextWithdrawal

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: ManagedNFTs mutation during active withdrawal cycle without adjusting nextWithdrawal
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: nextWithdrawal > ManagedNFTs.length makes the reset condition unreachable

analysis_questions: Does this block all future withdrawal cycles?
Is there any way to reset nextWithdrawal without completing the cycle?
validation_requirements: Start withdrawal for 3 of 4 NFTs (nextWithdrawal=3), release 2 NFTs, verify nextWithdrawal > ManagedNFTs.length
Call withdrawFromManagedNFTs and verify cycle never completes

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Owner starts withdrawing from 4 NFTs with numWithdrawals=2 (nextWithdrawal=2). Owner then releases NFTs at index 0 and 1 (swap-and-pop reduces array to 2). Now nextWithdrawal=2 == ManagedNFTs.length=2, which actually completes the cycle correctly. But if owner releases NFT at index 3 (last) while nextWithdrawal=2, array becomes [0,1,2], length=3, and cycle continues normally. The dangerous case: release at index < nextWithdrawal before cycle completes, making length < nextWithdrawal.
prerequisites: Specific timing of release vs withdrawal state

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Revenue collection permanently blocked; managed NFT revenue inaccessible
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All token holders lose access to future distributions
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: May require adding NFTs back to reach nextWithdrawal threshold

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No tests for ManagedNFTs modification during active withdrawal cycle

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by monitoring nextWithdrawal vs ManagedNFTs.length

validation_result.mechanism: withdrawal cursor corruption via array mutation
validation_result.impact_summary: Releasing managed NFTs during a partial withdrawal cycle can permanently stall the withdrawal process by making nextWithdrawal > ManagedNFTs.length, preventing revenue collection from all remaining managed NFTs.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The hypothesis correctly identifies a code defect in the interaction between releaseManagedNFT's swap-and-pop array mutation and the nextWithdrawal cursor used by withdrawFromManagedNFTs. When releaseManagedNFT is called while nextWithdrawal > 0 (a partial withdrawal is in progress), two issues arise: (1) An NFT swapped from the end of the array to an index < nextWithdrawal is skipped for the current withdrawal cycle. (2) If enough NFTs are released such that ManagedNFTs.length drops below nextWithdrawal, the completion condition `nextWithdrawal == ManagedNFTs.length` becomes unreachable. In this state, every subsequent call to withdrawFromManagedNFTs computes `limit = Math.min(numWithdrawals + nextWithdrawal, ManagedNFTs.length)` which is always <= ManagedNFTs.length < nextWithdrawal, so the loop body never executes, and nextWithdrawal never resets to 0. The WithdrawalStarted event is only emitted when nextWithdrawal == 0, so it never fires again either. The only recovery path is for the owner to call addManagedNFT enough times to make ManagedNFTs.length >= nextWithdrawal, at which point the equality check can eventually trigger.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Direct code evidence confirms both the defect mechanism and the missing safeguard. releaseManagedNFT performs swap-and-pop without reading or adjusting nextWithdrawal. withdrawFromManagedNFTs uses a strict equality check (nextWithdrawal == ManagedNFTs.length) for cycle completion. Neither releaseManagedNFT nor addManagedNFT has a require(nextWithdrawal == 0) guard.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: No check for nextWithdrawal > 0. Swap-and-pop modifies ManagedNFTs without adjusting nextWithdrawal. If the removed NFT is at index < nextWithdrawal, the element swapped in from the end is skipped. If ManagedNFTs.length drops below nextWithdrawal, the withdrawal cycle permanently stalls.

When nextWithdrawal > ManagedNFTs.length, limit = Math.min(numWithdrawals + nextWithdrawal, ManagedNFTs.length) is always <= ManagedNFTs.length < nextWithdrawal, so the loop never executes. The strict equality check nextWithdrawal == ManagedNFTs.length never passes because nextWithdrawal > ManagedNFTs.length, so nextWithdrawal is never reset to 0.

calling_sequence: 1. withdrawFromManagedNFTs(2) called with ManagedNFTs=[A,B,C,D] (length=4). Withdraws from A,B. nextWithdrawal=2.
2. Owner calls releaseManagedNFT(A, someAddr). Swap-and-pop: ManagedNFTs[0]=D, pop → [D,B,C] (length=3). nextWithdrawal still 2.
3. Owner calls releaseManagedNFT(B, someAddr). Swap-and-pop: ManagedNFTs[1]=C, pop → [D,C] (length=2). nextWithdrawal still 2.
4. (Optional) Owner calls releaseManagedNFT(D, someAddr). Swap-and-pop: ManagedNFTs[0]=C, pop → [C] (length=1). nextWithdrawal still 2.
5. Anyone calls withdrawFromManagedNFTs(100). limit=Math.min(102,1)=1. Loop: i=2, 2<1 is false, no iterations. nextWithdrawal=2. 2!=1, no reset.
6. Withdrawal cycle is permanently stalled. All future calls produce the same result.
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The core issue is that releaseManagedNFT modifies ManagedNFTs.length without adjusting nextWithdrawal. In withdrawFromManagedNFTs, the loop initialization `for (i = nextWithdrawal; i < limit; ...)` with `limit = Math.min(numWithdrawals + nextWithdrawal, ManagedNFTs.length)` means when nextWithdrawal > ManagedNFTs.length, limit <= ManagedNFTs.length < nextWithdrawal = i, so the loop body never executes. The completion check `nextWithdrawal == ManagedNFTs.length` fails because nextWithdrawal > ManagedNFTs.length (strict inequality, not equality). This creates an irrecoverable state where nextWithdrawal is stuck at a value exceeding the array length.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided that would contradict this finding. The deployment script (contract-deployer.ts) sets MinDistributionPeriod=10 and deploys with empty ManagedNFTs, later adding them via addManagedNFT. This is consistent with normal operation where the vulnerability can manifest.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The scenario is realistic and requires only owner actions during a partial withdrawal cycle. Step-by-step: (1) ManagedNFTs has 4 entries [A,B,C,D]. (2) Someone calls withdrawFromManagedNFTs(3), processing A,B,C. nextWithdrawal=3. (3) Owner calls releaseManagedNFT for A (index 0): D swaps to index 0, array becomes [D,B,C], length=3. nextWithdrawal=3. (4) Call withdrawFromManagedNFTs(1): limit=min(4,3)=3. i starts at 3, 3<3 is false, no loop. nextWithdrawal=3. Check: 3==3 → TRUE, reset to 0. This specific case works because length equals nextWithdrawal. (5) More dangerous: After step 2, owner releases TWO NFTs. E.g. release A and B. After releasing A: [D,B,C] len=3. After releasing B: [D,C] len=2. nextWithdrawal=3 > 2=length. Now permanently stalled. This requires admin action during a partial withdrawal, which is possible with no guard preventing it.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH severity. If nextWithdrawal becomes permanently stuck above ManagedNFTs.length, the withdrawal cycle cannot complete. This means: (1) No new WithdrawalStarted event is emitted (requires nextWithdrawal==0). (2) Revenue from remaining managed NFTs cannot be collected by the ERC20 contract. (3) All future distributions will not include new revenue. (4) Recovery requires adding NFTs back to make ManagedNFTs.length >= nextWithdrawal, which is a non-obvious workaround. Even in the less severe case where ManagedNFTs.length >= nextWithdrawal after releases, an NFT swapped to a lower index is skipped for the current cycle, causing missed revenue collection.
recommended_mitigations: Add `require(nextWithdrawal == 0, 'cannot modify ManagedNFTs during withdrawal cycle')` to both releaseManagedNFT and addManagedNFT
Alternatively, reset nextWithdrawal to 0 at the start of releaseManagedNFT if nextWithdrawal > 0 (accepting partial cycle loss)
Consider adding a public function to manually reset nextWithdrawal to 0 as an emergency recovery mechanism
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy LiquidInfrastructureERC20 with empty ManagedNFTs. 2. Deploy 4 LiquidInfrastructureNFTs, transfer to ERC20, add via addManagedNFT. 3. Call withdrawFromManagedNFTs(3) to set nextWithdrawal=3. 4. Call releaseManagedNFT twice to reduce ManagedNFTs.length to 2. 5. Verify nextWithdrawal == 3 (via state inspection). 6. Call withdrawFromManagedNFTs(100). 7. Assert nextWithdrawal is still 3, WithdrawalFinished was never emitted. 8. Repeat calls and verify permanent stall.
uncertainties: The owner must actively call releaseManagedNFT during a partial withdrawal cycle, which requires specific timing but is not prevented by any guard
Recovery is possible by adding NFTs back to reach the nextWithdrawal threshold, but this is a workaround and not an intended behavior
additional_testing_needed: Test releasing a single NFT at index < nextWithdrawal to verify the swapped-in NFT is skipped
Test releasing enough NFTs to make ManagedNFTs.length < nextWithdrawal and verify permanent stall
Test the recovery path by adding NFTs back until ManagedNFTs.length equals nextWithdrawal
Test interaction with addManagedNFT during partial withdrawal (adding increases length, which could cause other inconsistencies)
